coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based reinforced hydrogen-rich water nanocomposite coating for storage quality of fresh-cut pear
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yihao
Qiu, WeiYu
Fang, XiaoQi
Li, Wenxiang
Sun, Yanan
coredata.dc:description: Designing environmentally friendly edible nanocomposite films with antioxidant functions is of great interest in the field of fresh-cut fruits. Herein, Pleurotus eryngii carbon quantum dots (PER-CDs) were prepared from Pleurotus eryngii by-products, successfully characterized and added to chitosan (CS)/hydrogen-rich water solution to develop CDs/CS nanocomposite films (CS/PER-CDs), which was successfully applied to the preservation of fresh-cut pears. Thereinto, the fresh-cut pears treated with CS/2%PER-CDs nanocomposite films had minimum weight loss of 3.38% and the best retention of moisture, color, soluble solids and titratable acids after 5 days of storage. Importantly, the fresh-cut pears in the CS/2%PER-CDs treatment group had the lowest relative conductivity (37.53%) and MDA content of 1.94 μmol/kg, which could indicate that the CS/2%PER-CDs nanocomposite films maintained good cell membrane integrity of fresh-cut pears. In addition, the CS/2%PER-CDs treatment group effectively reduced the maximum respiration intensity (51.67 mgCO2/Kg·h) and ethylene production rate (0.75 μg/kg·h) of fresh-cut pear. Therefore, PER-CDs can be added into chitosan-based coating film as a functional component to make nanocomposite films and applied in fresh-cut fruits preservation effectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrogen-rich water
Fresh-cut pear
Nanocomposite coating
Storage quality

Weight loss, firmness and moisture distribution transverse images
Color, relative conductivity, MDA content and cell microstructure
Soluble solids, titratable acid, respiration intensity and ethylene production rate
XPS characterization of the CS/PER-CDs nanocomposite films
The effect of nanocomposite films on the quality of fresh-cut pears
Weight loss, hardness and moisture distribution transverse images
Color, relative conductivity and MDA content
Soluble solids, titratable acid, respiration intensity and ethylene production rate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2018YFD0700303 ), the Shandong Province Technology Innovation Guidance Program ( 66812211118 ), the Shandong Province Modern Agricultural Industry Technology System ( SDAIT-07-08 ), the Breeding Plan of Shandong Provincial Qingchuang Research Team (Innovation Team of Functional Plant Protein-Based Food) , Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2022QC104 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of PER-CDs (A), Diameter distribution of PER-CDs (B), Zeta potential of PER-CDs (C), FT-IR spectrum of PER-CDs (D), XRD spectra of PER-CDs (E), PL spectra of PER-CDs solution at different excitation wavelengths (F), HaCAT survival percentage after incubation with different concentrations of PER-CDs after 24h of incubation (G).
XPS measurement spectrum of CDs. High-resolution peak fitting XPS spectrum of (A) total spectrum, (B) C 1s from CS/PER-CDs, (C) C 1s from CS, (D) O 1s from CS/PER-CDs and (E) O 1s from CS.
Effect of different treatments on the weight loss rate (A), the moisture distribution (B) and the hardness (C) of fresh-cut pear.
Changes in the appearance (A), ΔE (B), the MDA content (C), the relative conductivity (D) of fresh-cut pears treated with different treatments during storage.
Effect of different treatments on the titratable acid content (A), total soluble solids content (B), respiration intensity (C), the rate of ethylene production of fresh-cut pears (D).
Effect of different treatments on the microstructure of fresh-cut pear cells.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based reinforced hydrogen-rich water nanocomposite coating for storage quality of fresh-cut pear

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, 266109, Qingdao, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, 266109 Qingdao, Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingdao Special Food Research Institute, 266109, Qingdao, Shandong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qingdao Special Food Research Institute, 266109 Qingdao, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing environmentally friendly edible nanocomposite films with antioxidant functions is of great interest in the field of fresh-cut fruits. Herein, Pleurotus eryngii carbon quantum dots (PER-CDs) were prepared from Pleurotus eryngii by-products, successfully characterized and added to chitosan (CS)/hydrogen-rich water solution to develop CDs/CS nanocomposite films (CS/PER-CDs), which was successfully applied to the preservation of fresh-cut pears. Thereinto, the fresh-cut pears treated with CS/2%PER-CDs nanocomposite films had minimum weight loss of 3.38% and the best retention of moisture, color, soluble solids and titratable acids after 5 days of storage. Importantly, the fresh-cut pears in the CS/2%PER-CDs treatment group had the lowest relative conductivity (37.53%) and MDA content of 1.94 μmol/kg, which could indicate that the CS/2%PER-CDs nanocomposite films maintained good cell membrane integrity of fresh-cut pears. In addition, the CS/2%PER-CDs treatment group effectively reduced the maximum respiration intensity (51.67 mgCO2/Kg·h) and ethylene production rate (0.75 μg/kg·h) of fresh-cut pear. Therefore, PER-CDs can be added into chitosan-based coating film as a functional component to make nanocomposite films and applied in fresh-cut fruits preservation effectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fresh-cut fruits and vegetables are warmly welcomed by consumers around the world due to the portable and ready-to-eat attributes, and the consumption of fresh-cut fruits is taking up an increasing amount of the total consumption of the fruit and vegetable market (Basharat Yousuf et al., 2018). However, the fruit's own barrier could be destroyed during processing, such as peeled, cored and cut, causing the increase in respiratory intensity and ethylene production. (Francisca A Ansah et al., 2018). Ultimately, the quality of fresh-cut fruit rapidly decreased in a short period of time. The crown pear is one of the most consumed fruits in China, it has white flesh, small pit, and sweet and sour taste. However, fresh cutting will lead to oxidation of flesh and deterioration of quality. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a technology that can better maintain quality and extend the shelf life of fresh-cut pear.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are new zero-dimensional nanomaterials with particle size less than 10 nm (Seungjae Min et al., 2022). CDs have attracted a lot of interest in various applications due to the multiple routes of synthesis, in addition, there is also beneficial properties, such as easy modification, specific fluorescent properties, microbial adhesion, photostability, water solubility and biocompatibility (Wanmei Lin et al., 2022). More importantly, it also has antioxidant (Krishna Kanthi Gudimella et al., 2021), antibacterial activities (Shanshan Mei et al., 2022), low toxicity (Parya Ezati et al., 2022) and environmental friendliness (Shi Ying Lim et al., 2015). It has potential application for improving fresh-cut fruit and vegetable freshness (Linlin Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2022).

So far, efficient and simple CD synthesis has been developed and applied as an economically sustainable technology in many research fields (Alas Melis Ozge et al., 2020). Meanwhile, CD is composed of a carbon core at the center and functional groups at the edges, and it has been found that the synthesized CD may have different types of functional groups and different properties due to the different synthetic precursors. (Yanan Sun et al., 2022). In the UNEP (United Nations Environment Programme) Food Waste Index Report 2021, the fact that large amounts of food were produced but not consumed was noted, and that this discarded food had negative environmental, social and economic impacts. It is estimated that 8–10% of global greenhouse gas emissions are associated with unconsumed food. This discarded food waste can be used to synthesize CDs, a sustainable, high value-added functional material (Hanzhi Fan et al., 2020). Among the large amount of food waste, one of the potential sources of synthetic CD is mushroom by-products, including mushroom roots and immature mushroom feet from processing, which are rich in carbohydrates, proteins and fats. Using mushroom by-products to synthesize CD and applying it to food preservation research will improve resource utilization while reducing carbon emissions.

CDs were obtained from safe organic source (including food waste and by-products) and exhibit strong functional properties, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and low toxicity, which are expected to be used for active packaging applications (Seungjae Min et al., 2022). Therefore, in this study, PER-CDs using Pleurotus eryngii by-products in one-step hydrothermal synthesis was synthesized and characterized, and added them into chitosan coating solution to make CS/PER-CDs for the storage of fresh-cut pears, and investigated the performance of PER-CDs as functional antioxidant materials for fresh-cut fruit during storage.

The Pleurotus eryngii by-products and crown pear were obtained from fruit and vegetable wholesale market, Chengyang District, Shandong Province, China. The pears purchased from the market were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 24 h before being pre-cooled, washed in distilled water and dried (at indoor temperature for 10 min). The carboxymethyl chitosan used in the experiments with ≥90% deacetylation degree from Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. in Beijing, China. Hydrogen-rich water was prepared by ionization with Hydrogen-rich water cup.

PER-CDs was extracted according to previously described method with slight modification (Linlin Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2022). The CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis process using Pleurotus eryngii by-products as the precursor material. Specifically, Pleurotus eryngii by-products were first lyophilized in slices (−85 °C, 0.01 MPa, 36 h) and then ground to powder using high-throughput tissue grinder, and 1.25 g of Pleurotus eryngii by-products powder (30 mesh) was mixed with 60 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixed solution was transferred to 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined hydrothermal reactor, and then placed in drying oven (at 180 °C for 6 h). The resulting product was centrifuged at 10 000×g, the supernatant after centrifugation was collected and passed through 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was purified through dialysis bag (molecular retention capacity of 500 Da) for 48 h. The dialysate was lyophilized to obtain CDs powder, which were stored in a desiccator to prevent moisture absorption.

The microstructure of PER-CDs was obtained using the transmission electron microscope (TEM) (HT7700, Hitachi, Japan) at 80 kV. One drop of aqueous suspension of PER-CDs was added dropwise to a copper grid coated with carbon and scorched with an infrared lamp for 30 min until thoroughly dry. The diameter and zeta potential of PER-CDs were determined using nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZS90, Malvern, China). Photoluminescence spectra of CDs were performed using an F2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Ltd., Japan) and quartz cell (1 mm). The infrared spectra of PER-CDs were obtained using Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Nicolet islo, Thermo Ltd, USA) scanning PER-CDs in the wavelength range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 and used to infer the functional group composition of PER-CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) maps of PER-CDs were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (D8ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction maps were recorded at a scanning speed of 10°/min between 2θ angles of 10° and 80°. The cell toxicity of the CDs was evaluated through using different concentrations of PER-CDs (0.01 mg/ml to 3 mg/ml) incubated with human keratinocytes (HaCaT) for 24 h and observed the cell survival rate.

Human keratinocytes (HaCaT) were used to assess the cytotoxicity of PER-CDs based on existing reports with slight modifications (R. Harish et al., 2020). Cells were incubated in both 37 °C and high CO2 (5%) environments. First, HaCaT suspensions with cell density of 1 × 105 were inoculated into 96-well plates for 12 h. Second, PER-CDs at different concentrations (0 mg/ml to 3 mg/ml) were added and incubated for 24 h. Then, 0.5 mg/mL 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo (-z-y1)-3,5-di- phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT) was added and incubated for 4 h. Finally, all waste solutions were aspirated and dimethyl sulfoxide was added. The absorbance values were recorded at 490 nm, and the ratio of absorbance values between the treated group with different concentrations of PER-CDs and the control group without the addition of PER-CDs was the cell activity, expressed as a percentage (%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation nanocomposite films and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on ESCALAB 250 Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific,UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocomposite films on preservation of fresh-cut pears
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh crown pears were peeled, cored and cut into pieces which soaked in distilled water (CK), chitosan coating solution containing 0% (CS), 1% (CS/1% PER-CDs), 2% (CS/2% PER-CDs) and 3% (CS/3% PER-CDs) concentration of PER-CDs for 3 min. The soaked samples were standing for 10 min at room temperature in order to form a uniform coating film on the surface. Afterwards, the samples were stored at 4 °C. During storage, the samples were analyzed at 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weight loss, firmness and moisture distribution transverse images
ce:section.ce:para:
The weight loss rate was determined by the weighing method, and the samples were weighed once a day during storage. The weight loss rate was calculated by Eq. (1)
(1)
W e i g h t
l o s s
p e r c e n t a g e
( % )
=
M 0
−
M 1
M 0
× 100 %

Where M
0 is the mass of the sample before storage (g); M
1 is the mass of the sample after storage (g).

The hardness was measured with mass spectrometer (TA.XT Plus C, Stable Micro Systems Ltd, United Kingdom). Parameter setting: 5 mm probe with puncture speed of 1 mm−1 and puncture distance of 5 mm, and the hardness was the maximum force (N) required to puncture the fruit.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was used to observe the distribution and migration of water in sample during storage. Based on the research study methodology of Fan et al. (2021). with slight modifications, the tests were performed on Multi Exp Inv analysis software for pseudocolor imaging. After calibrating, the following MRI parameters were set, TR (time recycle) = 800 ms, TE (time echo) = 20 ms, number of scan repetitions = 8, slice thickness = 2.5 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color, relative conductivity, MDA content and cell microstructure
ce:section.ce:para:
The color characteristics of the samples were measured by the colorimeter (NR10QC, 3nh Global Ltd, Shenzhen, China) with the L, a, and b values to calculate ΔE, which represents the color characteristics difference between the samples and white calibration plate. Before formally measuring the samples, the L0, a0 and b0 values of the white calibration plate were measured with colorimeter, and then the L, a and b values were measured three times for each sample with different treatments, and the ΔE was calculated according to the following Eq (2).
(2)
Δ E =
(
L 0 *
−
L *
)
2
+
(
a 0 *
−
a *
)
2
+
(
b 0 *
−
b *
)
2
Where: L*0, a*0, b*0 are the color values of standard white whiteboard respectively.

Relative conductivity was measured according to the previously described method with slight modifications (Xia Liu et al., 2019). The samples were cut into discs of 9 mm diameter and 0.5 mm thickness using a hole punch. A conductivity meter (DDS-304A, INESA Scientific instrument Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China) was used to measure the conductivity of the sample solution L0 after soaking in deionized water at 25 °C for 30 min and sample solution L1 after boiling for 30 min and cooling to room temperature. The relative conductivity of the samples was finally expressed as the ratio of L0 to L1.

The malondialdehyde (MDA) content of fresh-cut pears was determined using the method of Chen et al. (2016). The absorbance values of the treated samples were recorded at 532, 450 and 600 nm, the MDA content was calculated as shown in the following equation (3).
(3)
M D A
c o n t e n t
(
μ m o l
k g
− 1
F W
)
=
[
6.45 ×
(
O D
532
−
O D
600
)
− 0.56 ×
O D
450
]
×
V t
×
V r
/
V S
× m
where V
t is the total volume of the extraction solution; Vr is the total volume of the reaction mixture solution; Vs is the volume of the extraction solution in reaction mixture solution; and m is the sample mass based on the fresh weight (FW).

The microstructure of samples was observed by light microscopy. Thin slices of pear samples of about 15 μm were placed on slides at room temperature and observed at a microscopic magnification of 400 × .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soluble solids, titratable acid, respiration intensity and ethylene production rate
ce:section.ce:para: Soluble solids (TSS) were determined using CN series digital refractometer NY/T 26.7–2014 for the quantization of soluble solids content of fruits and vegetables, and the data were expressed in terms of Bailey's sugar (°Brix).

Titratable acid (TA) was determined by the acid-base neutralization titration method. 10 g of sample was added to 100 mL boiled distilled water, 2 drops of 1% phenolphthalein indicator was added, which was titrated by 0.1 M NaOH solution until pink color appeared and not fade within 0.5 min. The conversion factor f = 0.067 (malic acid) is based on the consumption of NaOH titrant and the results are expressed as mass fraction (%) of the titratable content of the sample.

The respiratory intensity was determined according to Duan B. et al. (2019). with slight modifications. The increased CO2 concentration was recorded on 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 days using JFQ-3150 respirometer and the respiratory intensity was expressed as mgCO2/Kg∙h.

The ethylene production rate was determined using the gas chromatograph (GC-2010, Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with an RTX-1 column and flame ionization detector (FID). 1.0 mL gas sample was injected into the gas chromatograph using a syringe with the column temperature, vaporizer temperature and FID temperature of 120 °C, 150 °C and 200 °C, respectively. N2 at a flow rate of 40 mL min−1 was used as the carrier gas, and the rate of ethylene production was expressed as μL kg−1 FW h−1.

Experimental data were subject to one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Mean values and standard deviations are reported. Differences among mean values were determined using Duncan's new multiple range test at a confidence level of 95%. SPSS 26 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was used for all statistical calculations. All the experiments were carried out in triplicate unless it is specified otherwise.

The PER-CDs were well-dispersed and uniformly distributed spheres, the feature shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1 (A)). The particle size distribution of PER-CDs can be seen from Fig. 1(B), and it have the particle size of less than 10 nm (about 1.5–4.5 nm), consistent with Pacquiao M.R. et al. (2018). According to the zeta potential plot shown in Fig. 1(C), it can be seen that the prepared PER-CDs have the negative charge on the surface, which indicates the possible existence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of PER-CDs (Yingmin Wang et al., 2019).

Fig. 1(D) showed the FT-IR spectra of PER-CDs, five distinct peaks were presented in the spectra, namely a broad peak at 3278.04 cm−1, four narrow peaks at 2917.92 cm−1, 1591.31 cm−1, 1024.97 cm−1 and 1403.56 cm−1 relatively, which may correspond to the O–H, C–H, CO, C–O and CC stretching bending vibration peak, respectively. Based on these peaks, it is speculated that PER-CDs have hydrophilic and water-soluble due to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of PER-CDs (Songnan Qu et al., 2012). The surface of PER-CDs contains a certain number of oxygen-containing groups, which are usually located at the edges of PER-CDs. It is known from previous studies that these groups could provide hydrogen atoms, therefore, PER-CDs as hydrogen donors on unsaturated bonds may possess special ability to scavenge free radicals (Yingmin Wang et al., 2019). This ability is most likely one of the reasons for CS/PER-CDs coating could inhibit browning of fresh-cut pears.

Fig. 1(E) showed the XRD pattern of PER-CDs, a broad diffraction peak generated at 2θ value of 20.4°, which revealed the graphite structure of PER-CDs (Wen Liu et al., 2017). The influence of excitation wavelength on the emission intensity was shown in Fig. 1(F). As the excitation wavelength was changed, a red shift of the fluorescence peak was observed and the peak intensity increased and then decreased, with the highest fluorescence intensity observed at the 375 nm excitation wavelength. The shift of the fluorescence peak indicated narrower particle size distribution or fewer energy paths (Shanshan Mei et al., 2022). The PER-CDs solution showed the yellowish color under sunlight and beautiful light blue fluorescence under 365 nm light source.

The safety of PER-CDs should be a major concern before attempting to add them to edible nanocomposite coatings, as seen in Fig. 1(G), the synthetic PER-CDs were not significantly cytotoxic at concentrations of 0.01–2 mg/mL, and there was no significant decrease in the percentage of HaCaT survival (p > 0.05). Notably, when the concentration of PER-CDs reached 2.5 mg/mL, it began to exhibit slight cytotoxicity, which corroborated with the results of the subsequent experiments, where CS/3% PER-CDs coating treated fresh-cut pears, the higher amount of added PER-CDs could induce damage in fresh-cut pears, resulting in poorer fruit quality than the CS/2% PER-CDs coating treatment sample. More importantly, the survival rate of HaCaT was still 79% at the concentration of PER-CDs at 3 mg/mL. The results indicate that the synthesized PER-CDs have good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity at relatively low concentrations (below 2 mg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS characterization of the CS/PER-CDs nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative chemical composition and chemical bonding states of CS and CS/PER-CDs coatings surfaces were obtained by XPS analysis. The full-scale XPS spectra of CS and CS/PER-CDs coatings were shown in Fig. 2 (A), the CS and the CS/PER-CDs coating have three main peaks (284.8 eV (C 1s), 399 eV (N 1s), and 532 eV (O 1s)) and two main peaks (284.8 eV (C 1s) and 532 eV (O 1s)), respectively. This result indicated that PER-CDs were mainly composed of carbon elements. In addition, Fig. 2(B) and (C) showed the high-resolution peak-fitting XPS spectra of CS/PER-CDs and CS-coated C 1s respectively. The CS/PER-CDs-coated C 1s fit three sets of peaks attributed to C–C (284.8 eV), C–O–C (288.3 eV) and C–OC (285.5 eV), respectively. The CS-coated C 1s could also be fitted with three sets of peaks belonging to C–C (284.8 eV), C–O (286 eV) and CO (288 eV), respectively. Fig. 2(D) and (E) were the high-resolution fitted XPS spectra of O 1s of CS/PER-CDs and CS coatings, respectively (Suganya A et al., 2018). The O 1s of CS/PER-CDs coatings were fitted with two sets of peaks attributed to C–O (532 eV) and CO (533 eV). The O 1s of the CS coating fitted with three sets of peaks attributed to C–O (532 eV), metal oxide (530 eV) and CO (533 eV). The CS/PER-CDs coatings contain oxygen-rich reactive polar groups on the surfaces, which corresponds to the FT-IR spectrogram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of nanocomposite films on the quality of fresh-cut pears
Weight loss, hardness and moisture distribution transverse images
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 (A), the weight loss rate of fresh-cut pears was increasing in different treatment groups during storage, and at the end of storage, the fruit weight loss rate was 4.26%, 3.38%, 4.8% and 4.97% in CS/1%PER-CDs, CS/2%PER-CDs CS/3%PER-CDs and CS treatment groups, respectively, and the sample weight loss rate in CK (6.48%) was significantly higher than other treatment groups (p < 0.05). The fresh-cut pears with CS/2% PER-CDs treatment had lower weight loss rate compared to the other treatment samples, while the sample with CS/3% PER-CDs treatment had increased weight loss rate compared to the CS/2% PER-CDs treatment, which may be attributed to the toxicity of PER-CDs at concentrations greater than 2 mg/ml. The results suggest that the addition of PER-CDs at moderate concentrations (less than 2%) could inhibit the respiration and transpiration of fresh-cut pears, resulting in weaker water loss from the fruit (M. Sneha Nair et al., 2020).

The pseudo-color image of fresh-cut pears moisture distribution was observed by MRI, and the display colors indicate different hydrogen proton densities. Therefore, the moisture distribution of the sample can be evaluated by observing the pseudo-color image. The more the color of the pseudo-color image tends to red, the higher the density of hydrogen protons in that part and the more concentrated the moisture distribution; while the more the color tends to green, the lower the density of hydrogen protons in that part and the relatively sparse moisture content. Fig. 3(B) showed that the water content of fresh-cut pears tends to decrease during storage, the area of red and yellow areas in the image decreased and the area of green areas increased as the extension of storage time. On the 3rd day of storage, the red area and yellow area of CS treatment and CS/PER-CDs treatment of samples were significantly more than those of CK treatment of samples, which indicated that the fruit water content of these groups was higher than that of CK treatment group, among which, the red area of CS/2% PER-CDs treatment group of samples was the most extensive and fruit moisture content was the highest. On day 5, the CK group sample showed large green area with almost no yellow area, the CS group sample contained small amount of yellow area, while the yellow area of the CS/PER-CDs treatment sample occupied 1/3 of the total area, with the CS/2% PER-CDs treatment sample retaining the higher water content, indicating that the addition of moderate concentrations of PER-CDs also helped to form a better barrier and reduce water loss.

Fig. 3 (C) showed the firmness changes of fresh-cut pears in different treatment groups for 5 days of storage. The firmness of all samples tended to decrease during the whole storage period. On the 5th day of storage, the firmness of the CK-treated sample was 10.9 N, which was 39.9% lower than the value at the beginning of the storage. The firmness values of the samples from the CS/PER-CDs and CS-treated groups were significantly higher than those from the CK-treated group (p < 0.05), indicating that the coating treatment delayed the decrease of firmness during storage, and that the firmness change was related to the loss of water during storage. Among them, the samples in the CS/2% PER-CDs and CS/1% PER-CDs treatment groups maintained better firmness, indicating that the addition of certain concentration of PER-CDs helped to form barrier, thus reducing the metabolism of fresh-cut pears and maintaining firmness (Kai Fan et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color, relative conductivity and MDA content
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (A) showed the appearance change of fresh-cut pears during 5-day storage. Strong surface browning was observed in CK groups samples after 5 days of storage, the CS and CS/PER-CDs coating treatments samples retarded the surface browning of fresh-cut pears. More importantly, the CS/2% PER-CDs coating could improve the appearance of fresh-cut pears on the 5th day, the result consistent with the ΔE shown in Fig. 4(B).

Fresh pears are rich in phenolic compounds, which underwent oxidation reactions with the oxygen that invaded in large quantities due to fruit cutting, leading to the accumulation of oxidation products quinones, forming the accumulation of black and brown pigments by polymerization and oxidation, and the surface of the fruit showed yellowish-brown color. The variation of ΔE values of fresh-cut pear fruit during storage showed in Fig. 4(B). During storage, the samples in the CS/2% PER-CDs treatment group had the smallest ΔE values. This could be due to the presence of nanocomposite coatings slowing down the action of polyphenol oxidase, which was consistent with the findings of Roberta Passafiume et al. (2021).

MDA is one of the important products of membrane lipid peroxidation in plant cells and often used as an indication of the degree of cell membrane damage. During the processing of fresh-cut fruits, there will be inevitable mechanical damage, which would accelerate the accumulation of MDA in fruits, aggravate the accumulation of brown substances, and finally accelerate the browning process on the surface of fruits (Huanhuan Zheng et al., 2019). Fig. 4(C) showed the changes of MDA content of fresh-cut pears with different treatments during the 5 days of storage, the MDA content in all treatments groups showed increasing trend with the increase of storage time. The MDA content of the CK-treated sample and the CS/3% PER-CDs-treated samples showed an increasing trend, while the MDA content of the CS, CS/1% PER-CDs, and CS/2% PER-CDs-treated samples showed a trend of first increasing, then decreasing and then increasing. On the 5th day of storage, the MDA content of the CS/PER-CDs treated group sample was significantly lower than that of the CK-treated group (p < 0.05). The results showed that the coating of CS/PER-CDs could slow down the membrane lipid peroxidation in fresh-cut pear fruits, reduce the accumulation of MDA in the cells, thus delaying the browning process on the fruit surface. Relative conductivity is used as another index to evaluate membrane integrity, and relatively low conductivity usually indicates better membrane integrity, as shown in Fig. 4(D). The relative conductivity of all samples showed an upward trend during storage, but the relative conductivity of samples in CS/2% PER CDs treatment was significantly lower than that in other treatment groups (p < 0.05). More importantly, on the 5th day of storage, the relative conductivity of the CS/2% PER-CDs treatment sample was 28.7% and 14.74% lower than that of the CK and CS treatment groups, respectively. The coating of CS/PER-CDs could effectively improve the oxidative damage but no effect on the integrity of cell membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soluble solids, titratable acid, respiration intensity and ethylene production rate
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (A) showed the effect of different treatments on TA of fresh-cut pears, the TA content of all samples showed decreasing trend throughout the storage period, and the CS/PER-CDs treatment could significantly delay the decrease of TA value of samples compared with the CK treatment group, and the CS/2% PER-CDs treatment could delay the decrease of TA value to the greatest extent. On day 5 of storage, the TA contents of the CS treatment sample and the CS/1% PER-CDs, CS/2% PER-CDs, and CS/3% PER-CDs treatment sample were 3.9%, 9.0%, 10.2%, and 2.4% higher than those of the CK group, respectively. This result was consistent with the findings of Xichuan Zhai et al. (2020) in the study of fresh-cut apples. In addition, Fig. 5(B) demonstrated the effect of different treatments on TSS of fresh-cut pears, which also showed decreasing trend during storage, where the CK group differed significantly (p < 0.05) from the other four groups, and the TSS of CS/1% PER-CDs, CS/2% PER-CDs treated groups also differed significantly from the CS treated group on the 4th and 5th day of storage (p < 0.05), elevated by 3.6%/4.3% and 3.7%, respectively. This result suggests that coatings containing PER-CDs could decrease respiratory rate, which showed effects on the glycolytic enzyme systems and the accumulation of acids, with CS/2% PER-CDs treatment group had the best effect.

The changes of respiration intensity of fresh-cut pears with different treatments during storage were shown in Fig. 5(C). During storage, all treatment samples showed trend of increasing respiration intensity followed by decreasing respiration intensity, and on the first day of storage, the respiration intensity of samples increased rapidly due to cutting. The respiratory intensity of the samples in the CK, CS, CS/1% PER-CDs, CS/2% PER-CDs and CS/3% PER-CDs treatment groups was 86.44 mgCO2/Kg-h, 64.58 mgCO2/Kg-h, 59.37 mgCO2/Kg-h, 51.67 mgCO2/Kg-h and 57.26 mgCO2/Kg-h, respectively, with the respiratory intensity in the CS/2% PER-CDs treatment group sample being significantly lower than that in the CK and CS treatment groups (p < 0.05). Fig. 5(D) showed the changes of ethylene production in fresh-cut pears during storage. The ethylene production rate of all treatment groups increased sharply after cutting, and the ethylene production rate of CS treatment sample, CS/1%PER-CDs, CS/2%PER-CDs and CS/3%PER-CDs treatment groups were significantly lower than that of CK treatment group throughout the storage (p < 0.05). Among them, the CS/2%PER-CDs treatment sample reached the highest peak of ethylene production one day later than the samples of other treatment groups. On the 5th day of storage, the ethylene production rate of the CS/2%PER-CDs treatment group sample was lower than that of the CK and CS treatment groups by 54.5% and 21.4%, which effectively reduced the ethylene production rate and delayed the arrival of the peak of ethylene production. This indicates that the CS/2% PER-CDs coating treatment could effectively inhibit the respiration intensity and delay the peak of ethylene production in fresh-cut pears. The results of previous studies showed that chitosan coating treatment combined with low temperature storage conditions was more beneficial in inhibiting respiration and ethylene accumulation, thus delaying the aging and prolonging the storage period of dates (Yi Yu et al., 2021). The results of this study showed that chitosan coating with 2% PER-CDs effectively reduced the respiration intensity and ethylene production rate of fresh-cut pears and delayed the peak of ethylene production rate, thus maintaining good fruit quality.

Fig. 6 showed the effect of different treatments on the microscopic cell structure of fresh-cut pears. It can be seen that normal pear flesh tissues had intact cell walls and tight intercellular junctions at day 0 of storage. After one day of storage, the cell structure of all treatment groups showed changes, and the cells in the samples of CK treatment group showed slight water loss wrinkling and the intercellular gaps became indistinct. On the 3rd day of storage, the same phenomenon was observed in the CS and CS/2% PER-CDs treated group samples. After 5 days of storage, the cell microstructure of the CK-treated group samples showed severe water loss wrinkling, the cell wall gaps became blurred, and individual cells were indistinguishable, while the CS group samples also showed more severe water loss wrinkling. Thus, the CS/2% PER-CDs coating helped to maintain the cell structure of fresh-cut pears, which may be due to the fact that the nanocomposite coating treatment could retard the oxidation of membrane lipids, and thus could maintain the integrity of cell membrane to maintain the microscopic cell structure.

PER-CDs from the Pleurotus eryngii by-products were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis method, and co-blended with chitosan to produce green nanocomposite coating (CS/PER-CDs). More importantly, the nanocomposite coating was applied to the preservation of fresh-cut pears. The results showed that the nanocomposite coating treated with CS/2% PER-CDs could effectively preserve the color and moisture of fresh-cut pear fruits, delay the weight loss and decrease of titratable acid content and soluble solids content, as well as inhibit the production of malondialdehyde and the increase of cell membrane permeability. It also has the effect of reducing the respiration intensity and ethylene production rate of fresh-cut pears, so as to maintain the good quality of fresh-cut pears. This study provides a new idea for the treatment of mushroom by-products, and the prepared PER-CD may become a promising functional filler for the preparation of green edible coating.

Yihao Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing-original draft. WeiYu Qiu and XiaoQi Fang: Resources, Conceptualization, Data curation. Yanan Sun: Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Wenxiang Li and Yanan Sun: Resources, Data curation, Project administration, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFD0700303), the Shandong Province Technology Innovation Guidance Program (66812211118), the Shandong Province Modern Agricultural Industry Technology System (SDAIT-07-08), the Breeding Plan of Shandong Provincial Qingchuang Research Team (Innovation Team of Functional Plant Protein-Based Food), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022QC104).
